Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Problem: Mobile GPUs use tile-based rendering architectures but existing shading strategies apply uniform shading rates across all tiles, wasting compute on low-complexity regions and undersampling high-detail areas.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Existing Methods: Variable Rate Shading (VRS) on desktop GPUs allows per-tile shading rate control, but mobile implementations are limited. Existing mobile approaches include simple screen-space heuristics for shading rate selection and fixed-function MSAA.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Motivation: Mobile GPUs have strict power and bandwidth budgets. By analyzing per-tile content complexity (edge density, material variation, motion vectors) at minimal cost, we can dynamically assign shading rates that concentrate compute on visually important regions while saving power on uniform areas.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Proposed Method: We propose a lightweight tile complexity analysis pass that runs before the main shading pass. For each tile, we compute a complexity score based on depth discontinuities, normal variation, and temporal motion vectors from the previous frame. Tiles with low complexity use coarse shading (1 shade per 2x2 or 4x4 pixels), while high-complexity tiles use full-rate shading. The complexity analysis uses a small compute shader that operates on the G-buffer from the previous frame, adding minimal overhead. We also introduce a temporal stability mechanism that smoothly transitions shading rates across frames to avoid flickering.
Adaptive Tile-Based Shading for Mobile G.Experiment Plan: Test on mobile devices (Snapdragon 8 Gen 2, Apple A17) using standard test scenes (Sponza, Bistro). Compare against uniform full-rate shading and existing VRS. Measure FPS, power consumption, and visual quality (PSNR, SSIM).

Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Problem: Mobile games often render at reduced resolution to meet frame rate targets, resulting in blurry or aliased output. Existing temporal upsampling methods like DLSS and FSR are designed for desktop GPUs and are too expensive for mobile.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Existing Methods: AMD FSR 1.0 provides spatial upsampling but lacks temporal stability. NVIDIA DLSS uses a heavyweight neural network requiring tensor cores. Mobile engines typically use simple bilinear or bicubic upsampling with optional FXAA.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Motivation: By designing a temporal upsampling algorithm specifically for mobile GPU architectures, we can achieve high-quality upsampling with minimal compute overhead. The key insight is to leverage jittered rendering combined with a lightweight recurrent filter that accumulates sub-pixel detail across frames.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Proposed Method: We propose MobileSR, a temporal super-resolution method for mobile. Each frame is rendered at a lower internal resolution with per-frame sub-pixel jitter. A lightweight compute shader accumulates samples into a history buffer at the output resolution, using motion vectors for reprojection. The accumulation uses a confidence-weighted blend that detects and handles disoccluded regions and fast motion. For areas with no valid history, we fall back to a simple edge-directed spatial upsampler. The entire pipeline adds only 2 compute passes and uses ~1MB of additional memory for the history buffer.
Temporal Super-Resolution for Mobile Gam.Experiment Plan: Test 2x and 4x upsampling on game scenes on mobile devices. Compare against bilinear upsampling, FSR 1.0, and TAA-based approaches. Measure PSNR/SSIM against native resolution reference, frame time overhead, and power consumption.

Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Problem: Real-time global illumination on mobile devices is extremely challenging, yet the absence of GI makes mobile game scenes look flat and unrealistic.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Existing Methods: Baked lightmaps provide static GI but cannot handle dynamic objects or lighting changes. Screen-space methods (SSAO, SSGI) are limited in range. Probe-based methods like Lumen and DDGI require significant compute resources designed for consoles and PCs.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Motivation: We can design a highly simplified probe-based GI system that trades off some accuracy for dramatic performance gains on mobile. By using a sparse probe grid, low-resolution probe updates, and temporal amortization, we can provide convincing diffuse GI at mobile-friendly costs.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Proposed Method: We place irradiance probes on a sparse world-space grid. Each probe stores a low-resolution (4x4) octahedral irradiance map and a depth map for visibility. Instead of ray tracing, we render tiny cubemap snapshots (16x16 per face) for probe updates using simplified geometry. Only 2-4 probes are updated per frame via temporal round-robin. At shading time, we interpolate irradiance from the 8 nearest probes using trilinear weights modified by visibility. The system adds roughly 0.5ms per frame on mid-range mobile GPUs.
Lightweight Probe-Based Global Illuminat.Experiment Plan: Test on indoor and outdoor game scenes with dynamic lighting on mobile devices. Compare against no GI, baked lightmaps, and SSAO-only. Measure visual quality (compare against path-traced reference using PSNR, SSIM), frame time overhead, and power consumption.

Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Problem: Mobile games must balance visual quality, frame rate, and battery life. Current approaches either target a fixed frame rate or use simple throttling when the device overheats.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Existing Methods: Dynamic resolution scaling (DRS) adjusts render resolution to hit frame targets. Some engines use simple heuristics to reduce quality when battery is low. DVFS controls GPU clock speed but doesn't consider rendering workload characteristics.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Motivation: By jointly modeling the relationship between rendering parameters and their impact on both frame time and power consumption, we can make intelligent quality-performance tradeoffs that maximize visual quality under a user-specified power budget.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Proposed Method: We build a lightweight runtime model that predicts frame time and power consumption for different rendering configurations. The model is a small decision tree trained offline on profiling data from the target device. At runtime, before each frame, we query the model with current scene characteristics (draw call count, triangle count, overdraw estimate) and select the configuration that maximizes a quality objective function subject to the power budget. We use a feedback loop from actual frame times and battery drain to adapt the model online.
Power-Aware Frame Rate Adaptation.Experiment Plan: Implement in a custom mobile renderer on Android. Test on 3-4 mobile devices. Evaluate against fixed-quality and DRS-only baselines. Measure sustained FPS over 30-minute sessions, total power consumption, and visual quality stability.

Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Problem: Shadow mapping is one of the most bandwidth-intensive operations in mobile rendering, stressing the limited memory bandwidth of mobile GPUs.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Existing Methods: Cascaded Shadow Maps (CSM) are standard but multiply the bandwidth cost. Variance Shadow Maps (VSM) allow filtering but require floating-point textures. Screen-space shadow techniques reduce bandwidth but produce artifacts.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Motivation: Mobile GPUs have tile-based architectures where on-chip tile memory is fast but off-chip bandwidth is expensive. By redesigning shadow mapping to minimize off-chip memory traffic, we can achieve high-quality shadows at a fraction of the bandwidth cost.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Proposed Method: We propose Tiled Shadow Mapping (TSM), which computes shadows per-tile rather than per-light. For each screen tile, we identify which shadow cascades are needed and only fetch relevant shadow map texels. We store a compact per-tile shadow mask in tile memory for use during shading without additional shadow map fetches. For shadow map generation, we use a single atlas with tight per-light viewport packing. We also introduce temporal shadow caching where static shadow regions are cached and only dynamic casters trigger re-rendering.
Bandwidth-Optimal Shadow Mapping for Mob.Experiment Plan: Test on outdoor game scenes with directional and point lights on mobile devices. Compare against standard CSM, VSM, and screen-space shadows. Measure shadow quality, frame time, memory bandwidth, and power consumption.

Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Problem: Occlusion culling on mobile platforms is challenging because CPU-based methods add latency and GPU-based occlusion queries are expensive on tile-based mobile GPUs.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Existing Methods: Software occlusion culling rasterizes a depth buffer on the CPU. GPU-based methods use occlusion queries but suffer from GPU-CPU round-trip latency. Hi-Z approaches work well on desktop but are less efficient on mobile tile-based architectures.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Motivation: We can exploit the tile-based architecture of mobile GPUs by building a lightweight hierarchical depth representation from the previous frame and using it to cull objects entirely on the GPU using compute shaders, avoiding CPU-GPU synchronization.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Proposed Method: We propose a two-phase GPU-driven culling approach. Phase 1: A compute shader builds a hierarchical depth pyramid (Hi-Z) from the previous depth buffer. Phase 2: Another compute shader tests each object's bounding box against the Hi-Z and writes visible indices to an indirect draw buffer. The GPU then executes only visible draw calls via indirect rendering. Previously visible objects are rendered first as conservative occluders, then newly visible objects are added. We optimize Hi-Z construction for mobile by using tile memory.
Hierarchical Occlusion Culling with GPU.Experiment Plan: Test on complex game scenes (10K-100K objects) on Adreno, Mali, and Apple GPUs. Compare against no culling, CPU frustum culling, and GPU occlusion queries. Measure draw call reduction, frame time improvement, and power savings.
